---
title: "CRUISE: Making Roads Safer for Everyone"
output: pdf_document
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{titling}
  - \pretitle{\begin{center}\Huge\bfseries}
  - \posttitle{\par\end{center}\vskip 1em}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
setwd("/Users/steicyl/Documents/Research/RISC")
library(openxlsx)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
```

```{r, include=FALSE}
fatalities <- data.frame(year = c(2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020), rate = c(1.11, 1.10, 1.14, 1.10, 1.08, 1.15, 1.19, 1.16, 1.13, 1.11, 1.34)) # Federal Highway Administration; U.S. Department of Transportation, Traffic-related fatality rate per 100 million vehicle miles traveled in the United States from 2010 to 2020

property_damage <- data.frame(year = c(2009, 2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019), prop_damage = c(3957, 3847, 3778, 3950, 4066, 4387, 4548, 4670, 4530, 4807, 4806) ) # NHTSA (National Center for Statistics and Analysis); U.S. Department of Transportation, U.S. traffic crashes with property damage only between 2009 and 2019 (in 1,000s)

speeding <- data.frame(year = c(2006, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020), speed = c(13609, 13140, 11767, 10664, 10508, 10001, 10329, 9696, 9283, 9723, 10291, 9947, 9378, 9478, 11258)) #NHTSA, Number of speeding-related traffic fatalities in the U.S. from 2006 to 2020

#working with age data 
#-----rename columns
age <- read.xlsx("Age of driver table.xlsx") #National Safety Council estimates
age <- age |>
    rename( age_group = "Licensed.drivers.and.number.in.crashes.by.age.of.driver,.United.States,.2023",
           licensed_drivers = X2, 
           licensed_drivers_percent = X3,
             fatal = X4,
           fatal_percent = X5,
           fatal_rate = X6,
           crash = X7,
           crash_percent = X8,
           crash_rate = X9)

#-----keep relevant rows
age <- age[c(5, 10, 16, 17, 18, 20, 21, 22), ]

```

# Problem

*Did you drive to work this morning? Drop your kids off at school?* For many, driving is a daily, routine task. Yet every time we get behind the wheel, or ride with someone driving, we substantially increase our risk of injury or even death. Traffic accidents are a leading cause of death in the United States. Insurance rates, medical costs, and traffic delays are additional costs of accidents even in the case of nonfatal accidents. Although there have been gains in reducing these accidents, these rates are alarmingly high compared to other countries. 
The likelihood of these accidents vary by age and gender, but given the prevalence of these accidents, the population at large is considered at risk. Distracted driving, speeding or reckless driving, and impairment are the largest contributors to car crashes. 

## Learning about the frequency of accidents

According to the National Safety Council, drivers of all ages are at high risk of car accidents. Younger drivers are more associated with incidents related to distracted driving and drunk driving while older drivers are more associated with impaired driving. Below we observe the distribution of driving incidents by age group.^[We observe a shift at the age bucket 55-64 where the population makes up more of licensed drivers relative to their proportion of traffic incidents.]

```{r, fig.width = 10, fig.height = 5.5, fig.fullwidth = TRUE, fig.cap = "National Safety Council estimates, Author's Graphing", warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, cache=TRUE, echo=FALSE}
#graphing
#-----setting up the data
percent <- data.frame(age_group = age$age_group, licensed = as.numeric(age$licensed_drivers_percent), fatal = as.numeric(age$fatal_percent), crash = as.numeric(age$crash_percent))

rownames(percent) <- percent$age_group
percent <- t(percent[, -1])

# Increase bottom margin to fit rotated labels
par(mar = c(7, 5, 4, 2))  # bottom, left, top, right

# Original colors
bar_colors <- colors()[c(23, 89, 12)]

# Grouped barplot (beside = TRUE)
bp <- barplot(percent, 
              col = bar_colors, 
              border = "white", 
              beside = TRUE,
              main = "Percentage of Licensed Drivers, Fatal Crashes, and All Crashes", 
              cex.main = 1.2,
              ylab = "Percent",
              font.lab = 2,
              xaxt = "n")  # suppress default x-axis

# Add x-axis labels centered under each group
axis(1, 
     at = colMeans(bp),    # mean of each cluster
     labels = colnames(percent),  # age group labels
     las = 2, 
     cex.axis = 0.8)

# Add legend
legend("topright", 
       legend = rownames(percent), 
       fill = bar_colors, 
       cex = 0.8, 
       bty = "n")
```

\newpage




# Costs and Causes

Although infrastructure and vehicle safety improvements help, the majority of accidents could be prevented by safer driving behaviors.

We are generally aware of the many consequences to poor driving practices. Traffic accidents will raise your insurance rates, can damage your vehicle, put you in medical debt, and cost you your life. **Why then, do we still engage in these risky behaviors like texting while driving, speeding, or driving while drunk?** 

Changing behavioral patterns can prove to be extremely difficult. Especially since different populations are likely to violate traffic laws in different ways. Behavioral change is challenging because the link between our driving actions and their consequences is often indirect: accidents are probabilistic, and other drivers’ behavior also affects outcomes. This muddies our perception of individual responsibility while driving. Observe the Figure 2 below. 

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="50%", fig.align="center", fig.cap="Author's figure"}
knitr::include_graphics("fig2.png")
```

With two drivers, there are two connections; with three drivers, the number of connections rises to six. Imagine the tens, hundreds or even thousands of drivers on the road. Who really bears the responsibility of following the law or following best driving practices?

# CRUISE Mobile App

Leveraging insights from behavioral economics, CRUISE uses gamification and social nudges to encourage safer driving habits.

Addiction apps like Forest, SuperBetter, and Finch leverage games and social connections to promote positive behaviors. Following this model, we can gamify positive driving behaviors. 

Users will start with an empty garage. They can earn coins by completing driving trips in which they abide by the speed limit and do not open apps on their phone during the trip. 

* If the driver does not complete the trip, they will not earn coins for the trip and a beat up car chassis will show up in their garage. 

* Great driving practices like driving during non-rush hours, avoiding late drives, avoiding congested areas can be rewarded by offering additional coins for the trip.

* Trips taken using rideshare services will always earn rewards. The app can proactively suggest these trips during late or early hours.

* Users will be able to purchase cars to store in their garage with their coins. 

 \newpage
 
# CRUISE App Visual

```{r two-images-single-caption, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Please imagine the aesthetics match :)", out.width="49%", fig.align="center", fig.show='hold'}
knitr::include_graphics("fig4.png")
knitr::include_graphics("fig5.png")
```

We also learn from behavioral research that social pressure is much more effective than self regulation at changing patterns. User will be able to attend virtual events, Car Shows, where users can "show off" their garage. Friends and family will be able to connect their accounts so that their garages are visible. 

# At risk populations 
We observed that teens make up a large proportion of accidents relative to the number of licensed drivers. Parents will be able to link to their children's accounts and create a family garage. This creates an additional accountability measure for teens who are especially at risk. 

A senior mode with a simpler user interface can be made available. This can allow users to keep track of their families trips. Linking this to rideshare services could also be beneficial; senior users can easily book rides for appointments

# Limitations

This app will require location data to flag speeding. Although this could raise privacy concerns for users, much of this data is similarly used by major applications. For example, Life360 leverages location data to calculate estimate driving speed. 

Integration with ridesharing apps may require partnership fees or Application Programming Interface (API) updates.

One concern may be that there is a selection issue with this solution. Those that need the app the most are least likely to download it. The in-app rewards for inviting friends and emphasis on social connection (Car Shows, Shared Garages) could help circumvent this. Additionally, parents could select their children into using the app which would treat the most at risk population.

Despite these limitations, the app has strong potential to encourage safer driving habits and reduce road accidents.

\begin{center}
\section*{Join us in making our roads safer with CRUISE!}
\end{center}
